message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>Shelter-in-place and other confinement strategies implemented in the current COVID-19 pandemic have created stratified patterns of contacts between people: close contacts within households and more distant contacts between the households. The epidemic transmission dynamics is significantly modified as a consequence.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  We introduce a minimal model that incorporates these household effects in the framework of mean-field theory and numerical simulations. We show that the reproduction number
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  depends on the household size in a surprising way: linearly for relatively small households, and as a square root of size for larger households. We discuss the implications of the findings for the lockdown, test, tracing, and isolation policies.
                </jats:p>
title: A minimal model for household effects in epidemics
